content.problemStatement:
Chef makes 222 types of (square) pizzas:


A small pizza, with length 101010 inches, costing AAA rupees.
A large pizza, with length 151515 inches, costing BBB rupees.
His customers are wondering which pizza is more optimal to buy if they want to maximize the amount of pizza received per rupee spent. For comparing, we will assume the amount of pizza to be it's area.

Output ’Small’\text{'Small'}’Small’ if the small pizza has more amount per rupee spent, ’Large’\text{'Large'}’Large’ if the large pizza has more, and ’Equal’\text{'Equal'}’Equal’ otherwise (without the quotes).


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains 222 integers - AAA and BBB.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line Small\text{Small}Small, Large\text{Large}Large or Equal\text{Equal}Equal as specified.

sampleTestCases.output:
Small
Large
Equal
Large
Small
Large
Equal
sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : The small pizza has an area of 100100100, and costs 100100100, thus a cost of 111 rupee per unit area, while the large pizza costs 400225\frac{400}{225}225400​ per unit area. Hence, the small pizza is more optimal.

Small
Large
